verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Arrivals of Diogenes and of Syrianus.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: After a period of six and twenty months, when Montanus had gone away, there came Diogenes the Notary; but he brought me no letter, nor did we see each other, nor did he charge me with any commands as from you.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Moreover when the General Syrianus entered Alexandria, seeing that certain reports were spread abroad by the Arians, who declared that matters would now be as they wished, I enquired whether he had brought any letters on the subject of these statements of theirs.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: I confess that I asked for letters containing your commands.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And when he said that he had brought none, I requested that Syrianus himself, or Maximus the Prefect of Egypt, would write to me concerning this matter.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Which request I made, because your Grace has written to me, desiring that I would not suffer myself to be alarmed by any one, nor attend to those who wished to frighten me, but that I would continue to reside in the Churches without fear.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: It was Palladius, the Master of the Palace, and Asterius, formerly Duke of Armenia, who brought me this letter.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Permit me to read a copy of it.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
